title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: Across countries in 2024, the share of children under five years old who are stunted—defined as too short for their age—varies widely against GDP per capita, which is adjusted for inflation and living costs. The scatter plot places stunting prevalence on the vertical axis, ranging from 0% to over 50%, while the horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale from roughly $1,000 to $100,000. A strong negative relationship is visible: countries with lower income levels consistently show higher rates of stunting, while wealthier nations cluster near the bottom of the chart. Burundi occupies the highest position on the vertical axis, with a stunting share exceeding 50%, followed by Niger and the Democratic Republic of Congo in the 45% to 50% range. Other African nations including the Central African Republic, Malawi, and Somalia fall between 35% and 40%. In the middle of the distribution, countries like Pakistan, India, and Cambodia show stunting rates between 20% and 35% despite varying income levels. At the lower end of the stunting scale, European countries such as Belarus, Estonia, and Bulgaria, along with high-income Asian nations like Brunei, cluster below 5% stunting. The color-coded regions show that African countries (purple dots) dominate the upper-left quadrant of high stunting and low GDP, while European (blue) and North American (orange) countries concentrate in the bottom-right. The data shows a clear pattern where higher GDP per capita coincides with lower prevalence of child stunting.
